mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Mongolia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the heart of the continent. Mongolia is landlocked, sandwiched between Russia to the north and China to the south. The world's second-largest landlocked country 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Mongolia?
mapQuestions.answer: Ulaanbaatar (often shortened to "UB"), in the centre-north. About half of all Mongolians live in or around it. The COLDEST capital city on Earth, with winter temperatures down to minus 40°C.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Mongolia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Mongolian, traditionally written in Mongolian script (vertical, top-to-bottom). Today most Mongolian books use Cyrillic (the Russian alphabet) but the old vertical script is being revived in schools.

mapQuestions.question: How empty is Mongolia?
mapQuestions.answer: About 3.4 million people across an area five times bigger than the UK. With around 2 people per square km, Mongolia is one of the least populated countries on Earth (only Greenland is emptier).

mapQuestions.question: Who was Genghis Khan?
mapQuestions.answer: A Mongol warlord who in the early 1200s united Mongolian tribes and conquered an empire that became the LARGEST land empire in history, stretching from Korea to Hungary. He is Mongolia's national hero.

foods.name: Buuz
foods.description: Steamed Mongolian meat dumplings, filled with mutton, beef, onion and garlic. Eaten by the dozen at Tsagaan Sar (Mongolian New Year) and on any cold day.

foods.name: Khorkhog
foods.description: Mutton and vegetables cooked in a metal pot with HOT STONES (heated in a fire) added to cook the meat from the inside. A traditional nomadic meal made on horseback trips.

foods.name: Khuushuur
foods.description: Big fried meat-stuffed pancakes, sold from street stalls during the summer Naadam festival. Crispy, hot, hugely popular.

foods.name: Airag
foods.description: Fermented mare's milk. Slightly fizzy, slightly sour, very traditional. Served in big communal bowls in nomadic gers (yurts). Has a tiny natural alcohol content but is treated by Mongolians as everyday food.

foods.name: Süütei tsai
foods.description: Mongolian salt-and-milk tea: green tea boiled with milk, water and a pinch of salt. Served all day in every Mongolian household. Surprisingly warming.

foods.name: Boiled mutton
foods.description: Big chunks of boiled mutton or lamb, cut into pieces, eaten with a small knife. The most common Mongolian way to share a meal in the countryside.

scavengerItems.label: A "ger" (or "yurt"), the round felt-and-wood tent that Mongolian nomads live in
A small Mongolian horse with a thick mane (Mongolians are still half on horseback)
A Kazakh eagle hunter with a golden eagle on his arm (western Mongolia, Altai)
The endless steppe stretching to the horizon under a HUGE sky
The Gobi Desert and a sand dune
Throat singing or a "morin khuur" (horse-head fiddle) being played
A nomadic herder family moving their flock between pastures
The Milky Way blazing across the cold Mongolian night sky
A wild Przewalski's horse (the only TRULY wild horse species, only Mongolia)
Lake Khövsgöl ("the Blue Pearl"), a deep alpine lake in the north
A Buddhist monastery with prayer wheels (Mongolia is mostly Tibetan Buddhist)

geoMatches.description: The capital. Half of all Mongolians live here. A mix of Soviet-era apartment blocks, modern glass towers, traditional ger districts on the outskirts, and the giant Sukhbaatar Square in the centre.
A massive cold desert covering southern Mongolia. Sand dunes, dinosaur-fossil sites, two-humped camels, and the Flaming Cliffs where the first dinosaur eggs were discovered.
Vast rolling grasslands covering most of central Mongolia. Sheep, goats, horses, ger camps, almost no roads, the biggest emptiness many travellers have ever seen.
Mongolia's "Blue Pearl", a deep alpine lake in the far north (close to Siberia). Holds 70% of Mongolia's freshwater. Surrounded by reindeer-herding Tsaatan people.
The western mountain range, shared with Russia, China and Kazakhstan. Home of the Kazakh eagle hunters who train golden eagles to hunt foxes from horseback.
The 13th-century capital of Genghis Khan's empire, in central Mongolia. Today only ruins remain, but the nearby Erdene Zuu monastery (Mongolia's oldest) is built from Karakorum stones.

senses.smell: Woodsmoke from a ger stove, mutton boiling slowly in a pot, the dry crisp air of the steppe, fresh airag in a wooden bowl, the cold metal of a winter morning…
senses.hear: "Sain baina uu!", the soft thunder of horse hooves on grass, throat-singing harmonics, the swish of grass in the wind, the silence of the Gobi at night, the morin khuur fiddle…
senses.taste: Hot greasy buuz dumplings, salty-milky süütei tsai tea, lean boiled mutton, sour fizzy airag, sweet aaruul (dried curd snacks)…
senses.feel: Wool of a ger felt wall, the warmth of a ger stove on a freezing night, the rough mane of a Mongolian horse, the cold wind on the steppe, the bumpy ride of a 4x4 across rough tracks…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How does a ger work?
stories.body: A "ger" (called a "yurt" in some other languages) is the traditional Mongolian round tent: a wooden lattice frame covered with thick layers of felt and a canvas outer. It can be set up in 2 hours by a family of three, and packed up in less. The door always faces SOUTH. The smoke from the central stove goes up through a "toono" wheel at the top, which doubles as a sundial. Mongolian nomadic families have lived in gers for at least 2,500 years. Even today, around a third of Mongolians live in gers, including in the outskirts of Ulaanbaatar. Inside, a ger stays warm even at minus 40°C, because the felt walls trap the heat from the central stove.

stories.question: Who was Genghis Khan?
stories.body: In 1206, a Mongol warrior named Temüjin united the warring Mongolian tribes under one banner and took the title "Genghis Khan" (meaning "Universal Ruler"). Over the next 21 years he and his armies conquered most of Asia, from Korea to the Caspian Sea. His descendants kept going. At its peak under his grandson Kublai Khan (around 1260), the Mongol Empire stretched from the Pacific to eastern Europe, the LARGEST contiguous land empire in human history (about 24 million square km). The empire encouraged trade along the Silk Road, built a postal system, and connected Europe with China for the first time. Today Genghis Khan is Mongolia's biggest national hero. There is a 40-metre stainless-steel statue of him on horseback east of Ulaanbaatar.
stories.thinkingQuestion: One person and his descendants built the biggest land empire in history. What does that say about how single individuals can shape history?

stories.question: Why is the Gobi famous for dinosaur fossils?
stories.body: The Gobi Desert is one of the BEST places in the world to find dinosaur fossils, because the dry climate and unstable sand mean that fossilised bones are constantly being exposed to the surface as wind erodes the ground around them. In 1923, the American explorer Roy Chapman Andrews discovered the first dinosaur EGGS ever known to science, at a place called the Flaming Cliffs in central Mongolia. The eggs were laid by oviraptors about 80 million years ago. Mongolian palaeontologists have since found Tarbosaurus (the Asian cousin of T. rex), Velociraptor (yes, the real one is from Mongolia, not Jurassic Park's version, and was actually only about the size of a chicken), and many more. Some of the world's most complete dinosaur skeletons are now in Ulaanbaatar's Natural History Museum.

stories.question: What is Naadam?
stories.body: Every July, Mongolia celebrates "Naadam", the country's biggest festival, also called "the Three Manly Games": horse racing, wrestling and archery. Horse races involve children jockeys (5 to 12 years old) racing small Mongolian horses across the open steppe for up to 30 km. Wrestlers wear special open-fronted jackets and brightly coloured boots, and the matches have no time limit. Archers shoot arrows at a target of leather rings 75 m away. The festival has been celebrated for over 2,000 years, originally as military training. Today it is part competition, part family reunion, part national party. UNESCO put Naadam on its "intangible cultural heritage" list in 2010.

stories.question: Who are the eagle hunters?
stories.body: In the Altai Mountains of western Mongolia, Kazakh families have used golden eagles to hunt foxes and hares from horseback for centuries. A young hunter catches a wild eagle as a chick, trains it for years, hunts with it for about a decade, then RELEASES it back into the wild to live free. The eagle and the hunter develop a bond that some compare to a long working friendship. There are now about 250 eagle hunters left in Mongolia. The Golden Eagle Festival held each October in Bayan-Ölgii is a celebration of the tradition, with hunters competing in horseback eagle-calling events. A famous 2016 documentary, "The Eagle Huntress", told the story of Aisholpan, a teenage girl who became one of the first female eagle hunters in centuries.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Mongolia so far?
Have you tried "sain baina uu" or "bayarlalaa" yet?
Steppe, desert, ger, mountain, which has felt the most "Mongolia"?
Did you ride a Mongolian horse? How was it different from horses at home?
How does the size of the Mongolian sky compare to anywhere else?
The Mongol Empire was the biggest land empire ever built. How is it remembered differently here than in China or Russia?

wordSearch.hint: Mongolian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Mongolian horse
animals.description: Short, sturdy, with a thick mane. Mongolians have ridden them for 4,000 years. There are about 3 million horses in Mongolia (almost one per person).

animals.name: Sheep
animals.description: Around 30 million sheep across the Mongolian steppe, herded by nomadic families on horseback.

animals.name: Bactrian camel
animals.description: The two-humped camel of the Gobi Desert. Adapted to extreme cold AND extreme heat.

animals.name: Golden eagle
animals.description: Trained by Kazakh families in western Mongolia to hunt foxes from horseback. A 2 m wingspan.

animals.name: Mongolian gazelle
animals.description: Big herds (some over 100,000) graze across the eastern Mongolian steppe. One of the largest hoofed-animal migrations on Earth.

animals.name: Przewalski's horse (takhi)
animals.description: The only TRULY wild horse species in the world (others are feral domestic horses). Reintroduced to Hustai National Park after going extinct in the wild.

animals.name: Snow leopard
animals.description: Lives in the high Altai mountains of western Mongolia. Around 1,000 of them. Almost impossible to spot.
